items.headline: Top biotech deals in May 2026
items.preview: May 2026 saw significant multibillion-dollar acquisitions in biotech.
items.article:
Seven multibillion-dollar acquisitions occurred in May 2026, primarily focused on advancing research in rare and eye diseases. Notably, Angelini Pharma acquired Catalyst Pharmaceuticals for $4.1 billion, expanding its reach into neurological rare diseases.

Bayer's acquisition of Perfuse Therapeutics for $2.45 billion aims to enhance its portfolio in vision loss treatments. UCB's $2.2 billion purchase of Candid Therapeutics will bolster its pipeline in autoimmune diseases. Additionally, Eli Lilly announced plans to acquire four biotechs for up to $3.8 billion, enhancing its infectious diseases portfolio.

Why this matters: These acquisitions reflect a strategic shift in biotech towards addressing rare diseases and innovative therapies, indicating a robust investment climate in the sector.

items.headline: Bial drops pariceract after Phase IIb failure in GBA-associated Parkinson’s
items.preview: Bial discontinues pariceract development after Phase IIb trial fails to meet endpoints.
items.article:
BIAL has halted the development of pariceract (BIA 28-6156) after its Phase IIb ACTIVATE study failed to show efficacy in slowing progression of GBA-associated Parkinson's disease. The trial, involving 273 patients, did not meet its primary or secondary endpoints, although the drug was well tolerated.

Pariceract was intended as a disease-modifying therapy targeting GCase activity, a key factor in Parkinson's pathology. This setback highlights the challenges in developing effective treatments for genetically defined subgroups of Parkinson's patients.

Why this matters: The failure of pariceract underscores the difficulties in translating genetic insights into effective therapies for neurodegenerative diseases, raising questions about future strategies in this area.

items.headline: Roche bets on BTK degradation in $2.3bn Nurix deal
items.preview: Roche partners with Nurix to develop a novel BTK degrader for B-cell malignancies.
items.article:
Roche has entered a $2.3 billion licensing agreement with Nurix Therapeutics to co-develop bexobrutideg, an oral BTK degrader aimed at treating B-cell malignancies. The deal includes an upfront payment of $700 million and potential milestone payments.

Bexobrutideg targets the BTK protein for degradation, addressing resistance issues seen with traditional BTK inhibitors. The drug is already in a pivotal Phase II trial for relapsed or refractory chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL).

Why this matters: This collaboration represents a significant investment in targeted protein degradation, potentially offering a new therapeutic avenue for patients with B-cell malignancies resistant to existing treatments.

items.headline: Photoimmunotherapy Targets CD98 in Solid Tumors
items.preview: New photoimmunotherapy shows promise in targeting solid tumors via CD98.
items.article:
Researchers have developed a photoimmunotherapy approach targeting solid tumors expressing the CD98 heavy chain, integrating immunotherapy with phototherapy. This method selectively destroys cancer cells while sparing healthy tissue, addressing a major limitation of traditional therapies.

The therapy utilizes monoclonal antibodies conjugated to photosensitizers activated by near-infrared light, inducing localized cytotoxicity in tumor cells. In vitro and in vivo studies demonstrated significant tumor shrinkage and prolonged survival in treated models.

Why this matters: This innovative approach could revolutionize cancer treatment by enhancing specificity and reducing toxicity, offering hope for patients with aggressive tumors.

items.headline: Metabolic organization of macaque visual cortex reflects visual field topography
items.preview: Study reveals metabolic architecture in macaque visual cortex aligns with visual processing demands.
items.article:
A recent study investigated the relationship between metabolic architecture and visual processing in the macaque visual cortex. By aligning fMRI maps with cytochrome oxidase histology, researchers found that metabolic intensity correlates with retinotopic organization and perceptual specialization.

The findings indicate that areas of the cortex responsible for processing faces exhibit higher metabolic demand compared to those processing scenes, suggesting that metabolic resources are allocated based on functional requirements. This research enhances our understanding of how energy metabolism supports neural activity in visual perception.

Why this matters: Understanding the metabolic organization of the visual cortex can inform future studies on neural processing and energy demands, potentially leading to insights into visual disorders.
